questions.question: ASTM publishes E1004 (electrical conductivity) as a test method but E243 (copper-alloy tube examination) as a practice. The label matters because a practice:
options: always contains binding accept/reject limits, while a test method does not
applies only to ferromagnetic materials
gives definitive instructions for performing an examination without producing a numerical test result, while a test method yields a measured value with defined apparatus and precision
offers options and information without mandating any course of action
questions.explanation: A test method (E1004, conductivity in %IACS) produces a measured result; a practice (E243, E309, E426, E571) is a definitive instruction set that produces no numerical result. The last option describes a guide, such as E2884 — and ET practices typically point accept/reject upstream rather than containing binding limits.

questions.question: ASTM E309 has steel tubular products examined under magnetic saturation. Saturating the tube during the examination serves to:
options: raise the steel's electrical conductivity so eddy currents penetrate deeper
suppress the permeability variations of the ferromagnetic steel that would otherwise dominate the signal as noise
leave the tube permanently magnetized so indications can be relocated later
increase the fill factor between the encircling coil and the tube
questions.explanation: In ferromagnetic material, varying magnetic permeability swamps the responses you are trying to read. Driving the steel to magnetic saturation suppresses that permeability noise so flaw signals stand out. Saturation does not change conductivity or fill factor, and residual magnetism is a side effect, not the purpose.

questions.question: An in-service program must examine installed carbon steel (ferromagnetic) heat-exchanger tubes in place. The applicable document and technique is:
options: ASTM E690, the in situ practice for installed nonmagnetic tubes
ASTM E243, because condenser tubing is involved
ASTM E1004, measuring conductivity through the tube wall
ASTM E2096, remote field testing — eddy current's answer to carbon steel walls
questions.explanation: E2096 applies remote field testing to installed ferromagnetic heat-exchanger tubes. E690 is its counterpart for nonmagnetic tubes, E243 is a mill practice for copper and copper-alloy tube (not in-service carbon steel), and E1004 is a conductivity test method, not a tubing examination.

questions.question: After the 1988 Aloha Airlines fuselage failure, eddy current inspection of aging-aircraft lap joints and fastener holes became legally mandatory through:
options: Airworthiness Directives issued under 14 CFR Part 39, mandating inspections detailed in service bulletins and the airframe OEM's NDT manual
ASTM eddy current practices, which carry the force of law once published
SNT-TC-1A, which requires lap-joint inspection of all certificated aircraft
ASME Section V, Article 8, as adopted by the FAA
questions.explanation: An Airworthiness Directive under 14 CFR Part 39 is federal regulation outright; it typically mandates an inspection whose details live in a service bulletin and the OEM's NDT manual (Part 6 in Boeing manuals). ASTM standards stay voluntary until invoked, SNT-TC-1A governs personnel qualification, and Section V serves pressure-equipment codes, not the FAA.

questions.question: Within an employer's user standards, the document that distills the approved operating procedure down to a single part number — exact probe, exact settings, exact alarm gates — is the:
options: written practice
technique sheet or data card
purchase order
terminology standard, ASTM E1316
questions.explanation: The technique sheet (data card) sits below the operating procedure and pins part-specific details. The written practice governs personnel qualification and certification, the purchase order is what makes outside documents contractually binding, and E1316 defines NDE terms — none of them carries bench-level settings.

questions.question: A surface-scan procedure whose scope covers wrought 2024-T3 aluminum skin is handed to you along with an aluminum casting to inspect. The compliant Level II response is to:
options: proceed — the alloy family matches, so the qualification carries over
proceed after verifying the reference notch still reaches the required screen response
perform the scan but note on the report that the part was a casting
stop — the part is outside the procedure's scope, and the mismatch goes to the Level III for written disposition before testing
questions.explanation: Scope is checked first: a procedure qualified for wrought skin says nothing about a casting. An out-of-scope part is a stop-and-escalate condition — hitting the notch response on the reference standard proves nothing about an unqualified product form, and a report note cannot substitute for documented Level III approval.

questions.question: Tubing codes require the probe cable's type and length on the examination record, and procedures treat cable substitutions as controlled changes. The underlying reason is that the cable:
options: is part of the resonant test circuit, so its effects alter the system's amplitude and phase response
wears out faster than the probe and must be replaced on a schedule
determines the standard depth of penetration in the part
needs to be recorded only when the customer witnesses the examination
questions.explanation: Cable type and length affect drive, bandwidth, and the amplitude and phase of the response because the cable is part of the resonant test circuit — which is why it goes on the record and why substitutions require re-standardization or documented approval. Depth of penetration is set by frequency and material, and recording requirements never depend on witnessing.

questions.question: Phase separation on a tubing job would improve at a different test frequency, and the Level III agrees the change is worth making. Before the new frequency is used on production tube, the essential-variable rule requires:
options: a verbal go-ahead from the Level III, logged after the shift
a note on each affected report identifying the new frequency
a revised procedure that is requalified and approved — a new frequency is a new test
nothing beyond re-balancing the probe at the new frequency
questions.explanation: Frequency sets the standard depth of penetration and the phase separation between lift-off and flaw signals, so changing it changes the physics of the test: essential change means revision plus requalification plus approval before the next part. Verbal permission and report notes are not approvals, and balancing alone proves nothing about sensitivity.

questions.question: ASTM tubular-product practices require a standardization verification at the end of the examination even when every periodic check has passed. The final check exists because:
options: it doubles as the instrument's periodic calibration for the metrology program
each passing check only validates the product examined since the previous good check — without it, the last stretch of product is unproven
it lets any gain drift be corrected before the data are archived
specifications require checks in matched start-and-end pairs for the log
questions.explanation: Verification brackets validity: a passing check proves the system worked for everything tested since the previous good check, so the end-of-run check is what validates the final stretch of product. Job-site checks never replace the instrument's traceable calibration program, and 'correcting' drift with gain hides a malfunction instead of proving sensitivity.

questions.question: Reviewing a draft report, you find an indication listed with location, amplitude, and phase — but no disposition. It falls below the reject threshold and above the recording threshold. The report is:
options: complete — dispositions are required only for rejectable indications
complete once the Level III initials that page
incomplete — no indication leaves the report without a disposition in the specification's vocabulary: acceptable, rejectable, or recordable
best shortened by deleting the indication, since the part is acceptable anyway
questions.explanation: Verifying the report against the specification's reporting clause includes dispositioning every indication; a sub-reject indication above the recording threshold is typically dispositioned recordable so future inspections can monitor growth. Deleting required content is a nonconformance bordering on falsification, and an initial cannot stand in for the disposition itself.
